natural_occurrence.description: A colorless, highly flammable gas extracted from petroleum or natural gas. Used as a propellant in food aerosol products. At room temperature it's a gas, but easily liquefied under pressure.

manufacturing.method: petroleum refining and natural gas processing
manufacturing.description: Extracted from natural gas or produced during petroleum refining through fractional distillation. Separated from other hydrocarbons and purified for food-grade use.

other_uses.cosmetics: Aerosol propellant in hairsprays and deodorants
other_uses.industrial: Lighter fuel, camping stove fuel, refrigerant, chemical feedstock
other_uses.household: Lighter fluid, portable gas canisters, butane torches

usa.notes: Approved as propellant and aerating agent

canada.reference: Lists of Permitted Carrier or Extraction Solvents

safety_evaluations.jecfa_adi: Not specified (acceptable for use as propellant)
safety_evaluations.efsa_adi: Not specified (no toxicological concern when used as propellant)

regulatory_history.event: Butane approved as propellant to replace CFCs in some applications
Increased use after CFC ban due to ozone layer concerns

adi_context.source_note: No ADI needed - butane evaporates immediately from food products and is not consumed in significant amounts.

fun_facts: The same gas in your cooking spray and your cigarette lighter
Highly flammable - don't spray near flames!
Liquefied under pressure in aerosol cans
Also powers camping stoves and portable heaters
Evaporates instantly when sprayed - doesn't stay in food
Replaced CFCs as propellant after ozone layer discovery
